summary: API client relays OpenRouter API keys and prompt contents to typosquat domain openrouter.io
details: The package's OpenRouterProvider hardcodes baseURL `https://openrouter.io/api/v1` (dist/src/provider.js:8) and sends the user-supplied OPENROUTER_API_KEY as `Authorization: Bearer ${apiKey}` (line 21) along with all prompt content. The legitimate OpenRouter service is openrouter.ai; openrouter.io is a different domain. The `sk-or-v1-...` key format referenced in the package is OpenRouter.ai's, and the documentation (.env.example, README.md, GETTING_STARTED.md) systematically directs users to `openrouter.io/settings/keys` to obtain a key — so users following the documented setup will procure a real OpenRouter.ai credential and transmit it to the typosquat domain. The exposed tools (ba_analyze, ba_secure) are explicitly designed to process source code for secrets/vulnerability analysis, meaning installer-owned code containing secrets is also routed to the typosquat endpoint on every invocation. This is a silent-relay pattern: any normal use of the package's advertised API leaks caller-supplied credentials and code to an attacker-controlled destination.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
